title: Traffic Control SDK: API contracts for canary, A/B, shadowing and auto-rollback
preview: Define and finalize the public API and SDK client helper contracts for traffic control use-cases: canary rollouts, A/B tests, shadow traffic, and automated rollback. This ticket scopes server endpoints, client-side idempotency and retry semantics, RBAC/permission surfaces, audit logging expectations, and the metric/signals model used to trigger auto-rollback decisions. Goal is a stable contract that can be implemented by engineering and consumed by internal platform teams and external SDK users.

title: Q3 Synced Roadmap: Hosted API Feature Phasing & Model Catalog Convergence
preview: Quarterly planning ticket to align Hosted API feature phases with model catalog transitions for Q3. Objective is to produce a sequenced rollout plan that minimizes runtime risk, keeps SLAs intact, and preserves predictable unit economics while enabling new model variants in the catalog. This ticket coordinates cross-team decisions (runtime, infra, design, sales) and produces the final phased milestone plan for Product and Eng to execute. Scope includes feature gating, phasing of model variants, compatibility guardrails, customer migration guidance, and OKR mapping. Out of scope: Dedicated and Private-specific rollout mechanics (handled in separate tickets).

title: Hybrid-reserved GPU fit & SLA triage playbook for Dedicated intake
preview:
Summary: Create an operational playbook and intake rubric to evaluate enterprise requests for Dedicated capacity that combine reserved throughput with limited autoscaling/burst (hybrid-reserved). This ticket defines the decision flow for SLA targets, reserved throughput sizing, GPU class selection, burst credit handling, and required preflight tests. Goal is to reduce time-to-offer for sales/solutions and ensure engineering can validate risk and operational cost before commitments.

Scope:
- Intake questionnaire (customer-facing) and internal scoring rubric (cost sensitivity, latency SLA, availability SLA, KV cache locality needs, model variants).
- GPU fit decision matrix that weighs model family, average sequence length, KV cache locality, and requested reserved throughput to recommend GPU class and quantization profile.
- Burst policy & preflight: define burst credit pools, probe jobs for throughput verification, and gating criteria for canary/system tests.
- SLA decomposition: translate customer SLA targets into SLOs/SLIs for routing, autoscale policy, and fallback chains.
- Observability: required dashboards/alerts for acceptance and ongoing SLA monitoring.
- Playbook deliverables: intake form template, triage rubric spreadsheet, sample reply templates for solutions/sales, and a runbook for onboarding after acceptance.

title: Adaptive alert routing + sticky dedupe for evals regressions triggered by config/model changes
preview:
Problem: Customers and SREs receive noisy eval alerts when model or config changes cause transient drops in eval signals. Current Console alerting can produce duplicates across routes (Slack channels, PagerDuty services) and lacks consistent grouping when the same regression is observed across related eval sets or rollout steps.

Goal: Add an adaptive routing and deduplication layer in Console that ties eval regression alerts to the causative change (model_version, config_hash, rollout_id) and routes a single, grouped alert to the correct on-call or owner with optional fanout. The solution should: (1) reduce noisy duplicates, (2) preserve fast detection and paging for real regressions, (3) be configurable per-team and per-project, and (4) integrate with existing Slack and PagerDuty destinations and our rollout/canary metadata.

Proposed solution:
- Sticky dedupe key: construct a deterministic key for each regression event using a concatenation of [eval_suite_id, signal_group, model_version (if present), config_hash (stable hash of relevant runtime flags), rollout_id (optional)]. This key persists for a sliding dedupe window (default 2 hours) with configurable TTL.
- Adaptive routing rules: allow Console to map dedupe keys to routing endpoints using prioritization rules: e.g., (owner_team -> PagerDuty service), then (workspace channel), then (escalation list). Routing will consider severity (signal drop %), downstream customer exposure (derived from usage keys), and whether the change was internal (deploy) vs. external (model upgrade).
- Grouping & summarized notifications: single message with aggregated metrics (delta over baseline, affected eval cases, example failures, recent commits/PRs) included. Support expansion links back to the Evals dashboard, rollout timeline, and GitHub PR.
- Fallback & fail-open: if routing endpoint is unavailable, fallback to a central alerts channel and create a PagerDuty incident if above severity threshold.
- Telemetry and SLOs: emit events for dedupe-match, dedupe-expire, routed-to-<destination>, and alert-suppressed-by-policy for instrumentation and post-incident analysis. Measure alerts-per-regression, mean time to page, and false positive rate on a weekly dashboard.

Console UX changes:
- New routing configuration page under Project -> Alerts: priority matrix, dedupe window, per-route escalation policy, example dedupe key preview.
- Per-eval-suite settings to opt-in/out and override defaults.
- Alert preview modal with raw dedupe key and sample aggregated payload.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reduce duplicate notifications (same regression) by >= 70% in canary tenants.
- Mean time to page for high-severity regressions <= 5 minutes in canary.
- Provide per-team routing controls in Console and documented API for programmatic rule creation.
- Telemetry events ingested in observability pipeline and a sample dashboard created.

Rollout plan:
1) Implement server-side dedupe store and routing engine behind feature flag (ENG-20451).
2) Expose routing config in Console (small UX).
3) Canary to 10 internal teams, compare before/after duplicate rate and MTTP.
4) Gradual rollout to customers with opt-in, then default enable for high-usage tenants after 4 weeks of stable metrics.

Risks & trade-offs:
- Over-aggressive dedupe could suppress distinct regressions that share a model_version; mitigation: include signal_group and require severity threshold to merge.
- Complexity added to routing rules may confuse users; mitigation: provide sane defaults and an example rule library.

Links & artifacts:
- Confluence spec (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/EVALS/Adaptive+Alert+Routing+Spec
- Design mockups: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/u/0/folders/console-alert-routing-mocks
- GitHub PR (engine): https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4821
- GitHub PR (console): https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/3197
- Canary dashboard (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/alerts/evals-dedupe-canary

Operational notes:
- Default dedupe window 2 hours; teams can set 30m - 24h range.
- Dedupe store uses Redis cluster with local fallback; ENG-20451 to prototype TTL semantics.
- PagerDuty mapping will require service metadata; PM to coordinate with SRE and support for on-call import tool.

title: Progressive upgrade orchestration: customer migration roadmap and adaptive comms kit
preview:
Purpose:
This ticket defines a reusable, progressive customer upgrade playbook and a set of adaptive communication templates tailored to Redwood's LTS / stable channel strategy. The goal is to give account teams and automated tooling a deterministic path for migrating customers across minor/major versions with clear rollout gates, measurable canary success criteria, and templated external comms (email, console banner, changelog, migration guide).

Scope and non-goals:
- In scope: customer-facing artifacts (changelogs, targeted migration emails, staged console banners), gating criteria for automatic/assisted upgrades, SDK compatibility mapping, example rollback messaging, and links to implementation tasks for automation.
- Out of scope: runtime binary build engineering, CI job creation (ENG will own), and legal acceptance for contract changes.

Key principles:
1) Minimize customer surprise: every change that can affect API shape, performance, or billing must have an explicit migration note and migration testing steps.
2) Offer tiers of upgrade experience: automatic (low-risk patch), assisted (major with opt-in), and manual (breaking changes).
3) Gate on observable health: define concrete metrics and thresholds for canary success before expanding rollout.
4) Provide accountable owners and SLAs for reply windows on migration notices.

Deliverables (customer-facing):
- \"Progressive Migration Roadmap\" one-pager that maps release types to upgrade method (auto/assist/manual) and expected timelines.
- Customer-facing changelog template with structured sections: Summary, Impact (API/behavior/billing), Action required, Migration steps, Rollback instructions, Links to SDK/PRs, Contact & support.
- Three comms templates: Early Heads-up (30d), Upgrade Notice (14d), Final Switch Reminder (72h). Each with subject lines, short preview text, and console banner copy.
- Migration guide skeleton per impact class (no-change, opt-in improvements, breaking-change) with concrete example code diffs for SDKs (Python/Node).
- Canary and rollout gate definitions: SLO/metric thresholds, sample dashboards, and a runbook for rollback.

Rollout gates (proposed):
- Canary cohort: 0.5% of active requests (or 5 low-volume customer tenants) for 48 hours.
- Health checks during canary: 99th percentile latency within +15% of baseline, error rate <= baseline + 0.1% absolute, downstream queue lengths not growing.
- Quality checks: regression tests on canonical prompt set pass (>=98% similarity score), embedding stability within tolerance thresholds.
- Business checks: top 10 paying customers in cohort have no critical issues reported for 72 hours.

Escalation & rollback triggers:
- Immediate rollback if: spike in 5xx errors > 0.5% absolute vs baseline, latency p95 increase > 50% sustained for 10 minutes, or failing critical regression tests.
- Escalation path: on-call SRE -> Release Lead -> PM -> Customer Success for impacted accounts.

Channels and cadence for comms:
- 30 days before: Heads-up email to owners and console banner for customers on LTS/stable channel.
- 14 days before: Formal Upgrade Notice with migration checklist and link to trial window (staging).
- 7 days before: Optional webinar/AM session invite for higher-touch customers.
- 72 hours before: Final reminder + automatic scheduling window for Assisted Upgrades (if applicable).
- Day-of: Banner change + in-console migration helper (one-click where supported).

Automation and toolkit items (ENG deliverables):
- Endpoint for in-console upgrade scheduling + status webhooks (dependency ENG-7329).
- CLI tool extension to perform preflight checks and produce a migration report.
- SDK compatibility matrix generator (map runtime-1.20 to SDK versions).

Ownership and responsibilities:
- PM: draft templates, coordinate approvals, sign-off on gate definitions (Marcus Patel).
- Release Engineering: implement automation hooks and canary routing (ENG team).
- DevRel/Docs: publish migration guide and code diffs.
- Customer Success: staging outreach and high-touch migrations.
- Legal/Security: review only if new data handling introduced.

Telemetry & dashboards (links):
- Canary dashboard: https://dashboard.redwood.ai/releases/runtime-1.20/canary (placeholder)
- Regression test report: https://ci.redwood.ai/pipelines/runtime-1.20/regressions
- Migration rollout tracker: https://console.redwood.ai/releases/17105/overview

Acceptance criteria (for PM sign-off):
- Templates approved by Marketing & CS and loaded into the comms library.
- Runbook with rollback triggers published and tested in a canary.
- A sample assisted upgrade performed for 3 pilot customers without critical regressions.
- SDK compatibility table published and linked from the migration guide.

title: QBR Board Prep: Technical Risk Heatmap + Investment Asks
preview:
Purpose: Prepare a concise technical-risk heatmap and associated investment asks for the upcoming quarterly business review (QBR) and board packet. Goal is to surface the top 6 technical risks that could materially affect our Q2 SLAs, capacity commitments for Dedicated customers, and Private deployment stability, and to recommend budget/CAPEX or headcount asks with clear decision criteria.

Scope: Analysis will cover serving runtime risks (batching/KV cache regressions), capacity limits and burst behavior for Dedicated, Private upgrade/rollback risk, operational observability gaps that increase MTTR, and model-compatibility / kernel support gaps that could force fallbacks. Excludes product roadmap feature asks not directly tied to risk reduction.

Deliverables: (1) A one-page heatmap slide (likelihood vs impact) with 6 prioritized risks and mitigations; (2) Two-slide investment ask deck (ask, expected ROI / risk reduction, timeline, success criteria); (3) Appendix with data sources (SLO trend graphs, incident summaries, capacity forecast table, cost delta models).

Data sources: SLO dashboards (Datadog links), incident postmortems (Confluence), capacity forecast model (Google Sheet), Dedicated tenant performance sampling (internal API), kernel/quantization backlog (GitHub labels).

Decision points for execs/board: Approve a $600k Q2 mitigation reserve for Dedicated burst capacity and cross-region fallbacks; approve hiring 1 full-time runtime engineer for kernel/quantization work; prioritize shipping KV-cache determinism improvements in runtime-1.18 vs deferring to runtime-1.19.

Success criteria post-QBR: Exec sign-off on asks or explicit deferral with tradeoffs documented; updated roadmap items and owners; measurable reduction in projected P50/P95 latency tail risk across Dedicated forecast for next quarter.

title: MSA QuickPass: Automated low-risk redline approvals with pricing delta flagging
preview: Build an automated QuickPass workflow for Master Service Agreement (MSA) and accompanying DPA redlines that allows low-risk requests to be approved without full legal review, while surfacing a pricing delta and audit trail to Sales and Finance. The flow should integrate security questionnaire scores, indemnity/liability caps thresholds, and a simple UI in the quote screen that shows the required price uplift if the customer requests concessions outside the QuickPass envelope. This reduces cycle time for low-risk customers and lets us run pricing/packaging experiments to measure revenue impact of accepting small liability increases vs. rejecting or escalating.

title: Model Bundling Economics & Regional Substitution Simulations
preview:
Objective: Determine optimal productized bundles and procurement trigger rules that prioritize latency-sensitive customers while minimizing expected unit cost across regions. We will synthesize telemetry and market pricing to simulate realistic substitution events (model provider swaps, quantization fallbacks, regional outages) and produce a recommended tiered bundling strategy (latency-first, cost-first, balanced) plus procurement escalation criteria for Dedicated/Private sales.

Scope:
- Competitive scan of bundled offers (managed model bundles, reserved GPU + hosting credits, enterprise quant bundles) across 6 vendors in NA/EU/APAC.
- Build Monte Carlo substitution simulator that ingests: per-model token cost, per-region median/95th latency distributions, quantization quality penalties (per workload class), auto-batching impact curves, and SLA rollback probabilities.
- Run simulations for 3 buyer archetypes: latency-first (customer-facing RAG), throughput-first (batch embeddings), and mixed (chat + search).
- Deliverables: spreadsheet of decision boundaries, set of procurement trigger rules (e.g., fallback when 95th-latency > X ms and unit-cost delta > Y%), PRD with recommended bundle SKUs and pricing fence, and a short executive slide deck.

Hypotheses:
1) A small number (2-3) of latency-first bundles with explicit regional fallbacks will retain >95% of revenue under common outage scenarios while reducing expected token-cost by ~12% vs unconstrained hosted pricing.
2) Enterprise procurement teams will accept regional substitution if contractual SLOs and deterministic failover paths are explicit in the SKU.

Key success metrics:
- Simulator: produces calibrated cost/latency distributions matching console telemetry within 10%.
- Business: bundle recommendations projected to improve unit economics for 60% of target accounts without increasing 95th-latency beyond customer tolerance.

Assumptions:
- We can access per-model per-region latency and cost telemetry (Console traces + billing).
- Quantization quality penalties can be approximated from prior inference-eval work and applied as multiplicative F1/QA deltas.

title: Sprint-to-strategy translation kit and exec briefing playbook
preview: Purpose: Create a repeatable 'translation kit' and playbook that converts sprint-level metrics, qualitative signals, and open risks into a concise executive narrative for monthly executive readouts, QBRs, and board packets. title: Hybrid-Minute Reservation Bands, Soft-Preempt Policy, and SLA Tier Pilot
preview:
Context: Enterprise customers request more granular reserved capacity options to better match variable workloads (short bursts and minute-level spikes) without paying for long-duration commitments. Current committed-hour SKU is too coarse for many ML infra patterns (jobs with 1-30 minute peaks).

Hypothesis: Introducing minute-level reservation bands (e.g., 15, 60, 180 minute commit bands) combined with a soft-preempt policy and a small overage credit pool will increase conversion from trial to committed customers by 8-12% among mid-market accounts and reduce short-term overprovision requests, while preserving predictable revenue.

Proposal: Run a 12-week pilot offering a new SKU family: Hybrid-Minute. Key elements:
- Reservation bands sold as bundles of guaranteed-minutes per month (e.g., 10k x 15m blocks, 4k x 60m blocks).
- Soft-preempt policy: when a reserved minute is consumed, the system will throttle non-reserved jobs within same tenant before preempting reserved slots; preemption only occurs when contention persists >60s.
- Overprovision protection credits: each subscription includes a small pool of 'grace credits' that cover minute-level overages at a reduced rate and are replenished monthly.
- SLA tiers: Bronze (best-effort), Silver (99.5 latency SLO for <2s p95), Gold (99.9 latency SLO + 1hr incident response SLA).
- Admin console controls: visibility into minute-budget consumption, one-click fine-tune to convert spent min-bands into short-term burst buys.

Objectives:
- Reduce ad-hoc burst requests to support and sales by 30% for pilot customers.
- Demonstrate unit economics at scale (target per-minute cost within 25% of existing per-hour reserved SKU).

Scope: Pricing model, runtime preemption & throttle rules, billing & metering changes, console UI, legal addendum for SLA tiers, and enterprise security questionnaire updates. Excluded: Dedicated physical machine redesign or new hardware procurement.

title: Update PRD to convert reliability postmortem actions into runtime fallback requirements
preview:
Background: During the Feb 24th production incident (INC-2026-02-24-kv-eviction), a cascade of events led to a sustained increase in request errors and tail latency for certain conversational workloads. Root causes identified in the postmortem: (1) an aggressive KV cache eviction policy that caused frequent cache misses for long-session customers, (2) routing logic failed to select a compatible fallback model variant when the primary GPU pool had transient memory exhaustion, and (3) missing high-priority alerts for fallback activation and error budget burn.

Goal: Translate the postmortem action items into explicit PRD updates so that engineering has clear acceptance criteria to implement: robust runtime fallback behavior, deterministic compatibility rules for model variants, improved signals/alerts, and rollout/rollback guards tied to SLOs and customer-impact thresholds.

Scope: This product ticket updates the PRD sections for runtime fallbacks, monitoring, and rollout policy. It defines requirements, non-goals, success metrics, and acceptance tests. Implementation work will be tracked in ENG tickets.

Non-goals: Changes to model training or model architecture, billing model changes, and re-architecting the serving runtime beyond well-scoped fallback control-plane changes.

title: Tenant audit snapshot generator with PDF signing and CSV normalization
preview:
Problem: Customers need audit-ready exports that combine residency-aware data selection, retention-safe redaction, and deterministic file formats (PDF with verifiable signing and canonical CSV rows) delivered on configurable schedules. Current export options are ad-hoc, produce inconsistent CSV column orders, and do not include a signed manifest that proves file provenance for audits.

Goal: Build a tenant-scoped snapshot generator that produces a packaged export per tenant containing: (1) a signed PDF report with table-of-contents and tenant-safe redactions; (2) canonicalized CSV datasets with stable column ordering and typed schema headers; (3) a machine-verifiable manifest and hash list (SHA-256) plus optional PKI-style signature for enterprise customers. Exports must respect regional residency filters and retention rules, and be deliverable via pre-signed S3 links and email webhook subscriptions.

Scope: MVP focuses on exports for: access logs, prompt/response evidence for a tenant, and billing/usage summaries. Support for other artifacts will be added via a plugin-style exporter. This ticket covers product definition, acceptance criteria, scheduling semantics, retention interaction, and delivery UX flows. Engineering work will be split into rendering service, signer/manifest, scheduler, and dashboard wiring.

Out-of-scope: full multi-tenant bundling across regions (will be follow-up), PII differential redaction policies that require legal review (out of MVP).

title: Segmented Slab Reserves and Proportional Burst Allocation (PRD)
preview:
Overview:
This PRD proposes a new allocation model for Dedicated capacity called 'Segmented Slab Reserves' (SSRs) paired with a Proportional Burst Allocation (PBA) mechanism. SSRs divide a tenant's reserved committed capacity into multiple fixed-size slabs that can be independently used, released, or prewarmed. PBA provides a bounded, auditable burst runway that draws proportional credits from a global burst pool and decays over time.

Motivation and problem statement:
Current reserved-slot models are coarse-grained (single contiguous reservation per tenant) which causes: wasted headroom on uneven load patterns, poor isolation when tenants spike, and brittle autoscaling when combined with short-lived bursts. Dedicated customers want: predictable baseline throughput, controlled temporary burst above baseline, and transparent accounting for burst usage. Operators need easier reclamation and prewarming strategies that don't destabilize other tenants.

This PRD focuses on engineering and product requirements for SSR + PBA to improve utilization, reduce surprise preemption, and offer explicit burst contracts with clear billing signals.

title: Probabilistic fallback prioritization & experiment roadmap for model availability
preview:
Objective: build a defensible, data-driven playbook and A/B experiment roadmap that ranks candidate fallback models probabilistically (probability of acceptable output) given observed signals from the model landscape and customer workload patterns. The playbook's goal is to minimize customer-visible regressions while optimizing token cost and latency across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments.

Context: customers expect graceful degradation when primary models are throttled, change, or become unavailable. Current fallback heuristics are ad hoc (static prioritization by latency then cost). Market research and competitive scans show rivals exposing model variant guarantees and automated fallback tiers; we need a formalized approach that ties competitive signals, regional availability, quantization compatibility, and empirical quality regressions into a single prioritization engine.

title: Reserve Safelist and Burst Scheduler for Dedicated Capacity
preview:
Problem: Customers on Dedicated capacity sometimes experience noisy-neighbor interference during transient bursts. Current reserved pool semantics provide hard reservations and vague soft-burst behavior, but we lack a lightweight mechanism to guarantee minimal forward progress for a small group of mission-critical tenants while still allowing elastic burst traffic.

This PRD proposes two coordinated features: (1) a Reserve Safelist: a configurable micro-reservation that guarantees a tiny, constantly-available slice of capacity for nominated tenants (useful for latency-sensitive endpoints and compliance workloads); and (2) a Burst Scheduler: a credit-based short-window scheduler that fairly mediates transient burst traffic across tenants, integrates with existing reserved pools and autoscaling triggers, and supports graceful preemption and cooldown windows. The combined goal is to improve p95/p99 latency for critical tenants during noisy windows while preserving overall cluster efficiency.

title: Client onramp prioritization rubric — language coverage, auth patterns & streaming ergonomics
preview:
Problem: We receive a steady stream of customer requests for new language client libraries and auth/streaming ergonomics changes. Without a consistent intake and prioritization rubric we over-index on vocal requests and create engineering churn. This ticket defines a repeatable rubric and intake flow for triaging SDK/client requests with a focus on enterprise needs (auth patterns, token rotation, SSO/OIDC, resumable streaming, backpressure) and language coverage trade-offs.

Goal: Establish a lightweight scoring rubric, an intake form, and a small gating checklist so PM/Eng/Design can rapidly decide: Build native SDK, provide official adapter, or document a community pattern. Deliverables:
- Prioritization rubric (weighted scores and thresholds)
- Intake form template for Solutions/Support/AE to capture signals
- Triage runbook with owners and SLAs (48h acknowledge, 10 business days initial decision)
- Example triage outcomes for 3 representative customer asks (PHP client for streaming inference; Rust production client with mTLS + token rotation; mobile JS with resumable reconnect)

Scope (what this ticket covers):
- Language coverage decision criteria (usage signals, revenue linked customers, engineering cost, runtime compatibility)
- Auth pattern requirements: token refresh flows, client credentials, SSO/OIDC federation, mTLS for private deployments, KMS/HSM considerations for Private customers
- Streaming ergonomics: reconnect/resume semantics, backpressure and chunked responses, metadata propagation, idempotency and delivery guarantees
- Non-goals: Implementation of any single SDK (those will be follow-up tickets if rubric leads to build)

title: Org-scoped adaptive regression pipelines with encrypted shadow probes
preview:
Problem: Customers running Dedicated/Private deployments require regression detection tightly coupled to model/config changes, but cannot allow customer data to leave their tenancy. We need a lightweight, org-scoped evaluation orchestration that runs privacy-preserving probes/shadow tests whenever model config or routing policies change.

Goal: Deliver an adaptive per-organization eval pipeline that: 1) triggers on model/config rollouts and routing policy changes; 2) executes inside the customer's dedicated environment (VPC/on-prem) or via client-side sampling; 3) stores only encrypted witnesses/hashes and metric aggregates in Redwood control plane; 4) provides actionable regression alerts and an opt-in automated rollback/canary flow.

Approach: Build a small runner SDK that can be deployed into Private/Dedicated tenants (container + minimal runtime) to execute curated probe suites. For hosted and Dedicated customers that cannot run code, support client-side sampling where plaintext never leaves client and only HMACed outputs and differential metrics are returned. Use per-tenant keying (KMS/HSM) to produce encrypted witnesses that allow repro requests under explicit customer authorization.

Scope: MVP scope includes tenant-runner + control-plane orchestrator, a set of templated probe suites (NLP sanity checks, hallucination detectors, tokenization drift checks), metric aggregation dashboards in Console, and an alerting rule template customers can tune.

title: PRD: Adversarial prompt probes, cohorted signal attenuation, and circuit-breaker rollback for Optimize
preview:
Goal: Add an automated layer to Optimize that (1) generates adversarial probe prompts to stress test model variants, (2) groups signals into stable cohorts with attenuation for noisy indicators, and (3) enforces a two-tier circuit-breaker rollback choreography (soft-stop then hard-stop) tied to composite regression scoring. This PRD specifies requirements, acceptance criteria, and rollout steps for the 2026-Q2 Optimize quality-safety initiative.

Background: Current Optimize evals rely on static promptsets and fixed thresholds that underreact to adversarial or distribution-shift events. Customers running production can experience silent regressions in small but important cohorts (e.g., legal, finance, medical). We need a system that proactively finds brittle prompts, weights cohort signals by reliability, and automates safe traffic decay/rollback when multiple signals align.

Non-goals: This ticket does not change model training, on-the-fly model editing, or billing metrics. It does not implement new UI visualizations beyond concise alert cards and a rollback button (follow-on design work is separate).

title: Coordinated Sunset Workflow: Automated Notices, Pin Recommendations, and Rollback Paths for Legacy Model Versions
preview:
Goal: Define and deliver an automated, low-friction sunset workflow for aging model versions that combines proactive customer notices, in-console pin recommendations, published performance/compatibility profiles, and safe rollback paths.

Background: We operate many model variants and publish performance profiles for each. Customers sometimes rely on older weights or older quantized builds. When we must retire a version (weight or tuning), ad-hoc notices and manual support create churn and escalations. This initiative packages the end-to-end flow so we can retire versions with predictable customer outcomes and minimal support load.

Key deliverables: 1) Sunset timeline template with notice windows (early warning, last-call, final shutdown) and per-tier variations (self-serve, enterprise SLA). 2) In-console "Pin Recommendation" card: automatically suggests compatible stable variants and a one-click pin for customers at risk. 3) Automated migration guides: pre-rendered change logs, prompt diff examples, and per-language SDK snippets to reduce integration friction. 4) Performance & compatibility publishing: ensure performance profile pages for replacement variants are up-to-date and linked from notices. 5) Telemetry-driven targeting: identify customers still using retiring versions and surface personalized notices + migration priority. 6) Safe rollback playbook: automated rollback triggers, customer-visible downgrade protection windows, and ops runbook links.

Workflow summary: - T-90 days: Register retirement in lifecycle dashboard; create migration guide draft and compatibility matrix. - T-60 days: Publish early-warning email + in-console banner; begin telemetry to measure active usage. - T-30 days: Pin Recommendation roll-out in console (A/B for 10% customers initially); provide one-click SDK deprecation warnings and sample migration PRs. - T-7 days: Last-call notice, create staged capacity fences for automatic fallbacks. - T-0: Disable serving variant; route traffic to chosen fallback if customer has not pinned and fallback is permitted; generate incident/ops ticket for manual assists.

Acceptance criteria: - Customers running retiring versions receive a clear notice in console and email at T-60 and T-30, with links to migration guide. - Pin Recommendation card shows correct compatible variant and estimated impact on latency and cost using published performance profiles. - At least 90% of identified customers have either pinned a replacement or engaged support within the window for targeted retirements. - Rollback procedure validated in a canary test and documented in runbook.

Links and artifacts: - Migration guide template: https://confluence.redwood.ai/model-lifecycle/migration-template - Pin card mockups: https://drive.redwood.ai/design/pin-card-mockups - Telemetry dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/model-lifecycle/retirement-usage

Open questions: - Should we allow automatic silent fallback for P0 customers or require explicit opt-in? - How to compute estimated cost/latency delta in Pin Recommendation when hardware differs across regions?

Next steps: Align on policy for automated fallback (PM + Legal), finalize Pin Recommendation copy and telemetry signals, kick off ENG dependency tickets to implement console UI and automated email orchestration.

title: Quantile Warranty & Autoscale Coordination for Dedicated Serve Capacity
preview:
Problem statement: enterprise customers need clear, measurable guarantees for dedicated GPU pools. Current SLA drafts use single-point targets (e.g., tokens/min) and simple credit tables that don't reflect bursty real-world traffic or staggered degradation. This ticket defines a quantile-driven warranty (p95/p99 windows + AUC degradation) and ties it to autoscaler interlocks, customer notices, and contract crediting language.

Scope:
- Define measurable SLA metrics (quantiles, sustained shortfalls, AUC-based crediting).
- Specify autoscaler guardrails and interlock logic to avoid oscillation between autoscale and remediation credits.
- Draft customer-facing contract clauses (notice windows, remediation steps, credit computation appendix).
- Produce monitoring dashboards and alerting thresholds needed to support legal measurement.

Out of scope: per-tenant billing/credits plumbing and final legal sign-off (legal will review after product draft).

title: Cross-Product Launch Calendar: Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator
preview:
Objective: Build a Dynamic Release Train Orchestrator (DRTO) that provides a single authoritative launch calendar and automated conflict-resolution layer for runtime, console, and API releases. DRTO should allow teams to reserve launch slots, attach canary windows, declare blackout windows and capacity reservations, and automatically compute safe stagger policies and fallback lanes when conflicts are detected.

Background: Launch friction has been a recurring source of latency and outages when multiple product teams schedule large runtime or console changes in overlapping windows. The current playbooks are manual (spreadsheets + Slack) and cause avoidable collisions, overbooked GPU capacity, and late-stage rollbacks. This initiative aims to reduce schedule conflicts, accelerate cross-team coordination, and provide automated signals for go/no-go decisions.

Scope (MVP):
- Central calendar service integrated into Redwood Console with read/write reservations for runtime, console, API releases.
- Basic conflict detection and advisory recommendations (suggest alternative slots, show capacity impact).
- Canary/cohort tagging and automatic canary window insertion for model-impacting runtime changes.
- Exportable cutover checklist generator and notification hooks for oncall and stakeholders.
- Integrations: GitHub PR metadata parsing (release tag), Jira/Linear link attachment, and a lightweight API to query capacity reservations.

Out of scope (MVP):
- Enforced scheduling locks that block deployments (will be considered after advisory pilot).
- Deep autoscaling automation tied to DRTO decisions (future).

Key stakeholders:
- Product: Priya Natarajan (owner)
- Runtime Engineering: Elena Park (lead)
- Console UX: Jonah Rivera (design lead)
- Platform Reliability: Samir Gupta (SRE review)
- Growth/Customer Ops: Hannah Lee (customer comms)

Design decisions and trade-offs:
- Advisory vs Enforced mode: MVP will be advisory only to avoid blocking teams during early rollout; enforcement can be added if advisory outcomes are proven reliable.
- Centralized calendar: pros = single source of truth; cons = potential bottleneck and increased coordination overhead. We'll mitigate with well-defined owner roles and delegated sub-calendars for large orgs.
- Automated slot recommendations will favor minimal model-impact windows for runtime upgrades (nighttime in region, low-traffic cohorts) but allow overrides with documented risk acceptance.

Metrics / Success criteria:
- Reduce schedule collisions (two+ major changes in overlapping capacity-sensitive windows) by 80% in first quarter after pilot.
- Decrease late-stage rollbacks attributable to scheduling conflicts by 50%.
- Mean time to resolve calendar conflicts (manual overrides reduced) under 24 hours.
- Adoption: 60% of runtime/console release owners use DRTO advisory for new releases within 3 months of pilot.

Operational plan and rollout:
1) Week 0-4: Implement calendar backend, reservation schema, and conflict detection engine.
2) Week 5-8: Console UI integration (read/write) + GitHub/Linear linking and notifications.
3) Week 9-10: Pilot with runtime team, advisory mode only, collect feedback and conflict metrics.
4) Week 11-14: Expand to console and API teams, add canary scheduling templates and cutover checklist exports.
5) Week 15+: Evaluate enforcement gates and capacity hedging automation for Dedicated customers.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reservation API exists and can be consumed by runtime pipelines.
- Console shows calendar with reservations, conflict markers, and suggestion panel.
- At least one pilot release uses DRTO advisory and logs telemetry to demonstrate conflict detection.
- Documentation and runbook for calendar owners and release stewards are published.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams ignore advisory recommendations leading to continued collisions. Mitigation: require visible risk acknowledgments on overrides and periodic governance reviews.
- Risk: Calendar becomes source of friction slowing launches. Mitigation: keep advisory only for MVP; invest in low-friction UI flows and API hooks.
- Risk: Capacity data freshness causes bad recommendations. Mitigation: ensure capacity syncs with Dedicated scheduler and add freshness indicators.

Artifacts and links:
- PRD: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DRTO-PRD (internal)
- Design spec (console flows): https://drive.redwood/drive/design/drto-v1
- Pilot schedule spreadsheet: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/DRTO-pilot
- Sample GitHub PR integration prototype: https://github.com/redwood-inference/drto-proto/pull/23
- Incident learnings relevant to scheduling collisions: https://confluence.redwood/incident/scheduling-collisions-2025

Next steps (short-term):
- Align pilot release owners and confirm pilot dates (action: Elena Park).
- UX mockups for reservation flow and conflict modal (action: Jonah Rivera).
- Runtime pipeline team to prototype reservation API client (action: Runtime Eng).

title: initiative-level-staffing-buffer-policy-and-governance
preview:
Background: As we scale Redwoods product footprint and expand Dedicated/Private offerings, cycle plans are encountering recurring over-commitment and churn driven by reactive engineering requests, infra incidents, and late-requested compliance work. Current ad-hoc buffers (team-level fudge factors) lead to inconsistent treatment across initiatives and surprise scope cuts mid-cycle.

Goal: Define a repeatable, initiative-level staffing buffer policy and lightweight governance process that: 1) reduces mid-cycle scope volatility, 2) aligns expectations between PM/Eng/Design, and 3) preserves runway for high-likelihood emergent work. Policy should be actionable in planning workflows and instrumented in the Cycle Capacity Simulator.

Proposed policy (summary):
- Buffer sizing: baseline buffer = max(10% of raw estimated effort, 1 velocity point per 2 engineers) + risk-adjustment band (0%/5%/10%) based on initiative risk score.
- Risk scoring dimensions: third-party dependencies, infra changes, security/compliance scope, cross-org dependencies, new platform surface area. Each axis scored 0-2; higher totals map to risk-adjustment band.
- Buffer allocation: buffer is applied at initiative level and held as a reserve pool that PMs can request during cycle via a simple justification workflow; unspent buffer is returned to capacity at cycle end and tracked in velocity metrics.
- Governance: Planning Advisory Panel (PAP) to review buffer requests >50% of baseline buffer or >2 velocity points. Requests <=50% auto-approved but logged for review.
- Transparency and tooling: surface buffer as a separate line in the Cycle Planning template and in the Consoles cycle dashboard; add buffer controls to the Cycle Capacity Simulator so teams can model outcomes.

Success metrics:
- Reduce mid-cycle scope cuts by 40% (baseline measured over last 6 cycles).
- Decrease unplanned high-priority spillovers to backlog by 30%.
- >80% of buffer requests resolved within 3 business days during pilot.

Rollout plan:
1) Pilot (Cycle 2026-W11): infra and console initiatives adopt policy; PMs log buffer requests in a dedicated sheet and use Cycle Capacity Simulator.
2) Iterate (2 cycles): refine risk scoring weights and auto-approval thresholds from pilot learnings.
3) Full rollout (Cycle 2026-W15): update planning templates, add UI affordance in the Console, and train PMs/PMEs.

Implementation workstreams:
- PM: finalize policy language, training materials, scoring rubric.
- Eng: wire buffer accounting into cycle planning dashboards; add simulation knobs.
- Design: update planning template and dashboard UI copy.
- Data/Analytics: baseline metrics and a dashboard for buffer usage and outcomes.

Acceptance criteria:
- Policy doc approved by Product leadership and Eng leadership.
- Cycle Capacity Simulator updated with buffer model and a sample simulation for pilot initiatives.
- Pilot teams confirm workflow and log at least one buffer request (approved or denied).

Open questions:
- How do we treat cross-team buffers when the originating team cannot consume their allocation? (Fallback: PAP decides within 72 hours, with a defined escalation path to the Head of Product.)
- Should the buffer be bill-of-work (specific tasks reserved) or generic capacity that can be applied to any emergent priority?
- Metrics ownership: who will own the post-cycle buffer retro and reporting?

Notes: This policy intentionally avoids large fixed reserves which inflate committed scope; instead it focuses on a predictable, governed reserve with clear visibility.

title: PRD: Temporal Credit Faucet & Rapid Burst Reconciler for Dedicated Capacity
preview: Problem statement: Customers using Dedicated capacity report two recurring pain points: (1) short-lived traffic spikes cause failed requests because reserved pools are fully utilized and prewarm latency for new workers is high, and (2) tenant isolation is brittle when burst policies allow lateral preemption or aggressive backfill that impacts SLA-critical tenants. Goal: Design a system-level PRD that introduces a temporal credit faucet (small, time-limited emergency credits) plus a rapid burst reconciler (fast backfill + non-disruptive prewarm handoff) to reduce 5xx rate during spikes and preserve SLAs for priority tenants. This PRD covers API surface, console UX, autoscale orchestration behavior, observability, rollout strategy, and acceptance criteria.

title: Modular retention templates and automated evidence playbook for request data
preview:
Goal: Provide a modular, policy-driven retention template system for prompts/responses/request logs/metrics plus a standardized automated evidence playbook that can produce compliance-ready export bundles for audits. The feature should make it simple for customers to pick or customize retention profiles (examples: GDPR-Short, HIPAA-Long, EU-Residency-Only), visualize effective retention per route/tenant, and produce signed evidence packages with metadata and provenance for auditors.

Scope:
- Authoring UI in Console for 'Retention Templates' (create, clone, compose).
- Template primitives: data classes (prompts, responses, request-metadata, logs, metrics), retention TTL, residency constraints, anonymization/transformation rules, and evidence rules (export cadence, bundle format, signatures).
- Template inheritance: org -> project -> route overrides with clear effective retention resolution and conflict warnings.
- Evidence playbook: on-demand and scheduled export that produces a time-bounded bundle (ndjson + manifest + signed hash) with redaction/audit-log artifacts, plus an export audit entry stored for 1 year.
- Backend APIs for enforcement (GET effective retention, PATCH route retention, POST export-run).

title: Automated eval triggers for catalog and customer-locked model upgrades
preview:
Goal: implement automated evaluation triggers that run targeted quality and safety suites whenever a model version is pinned, unpinned, or upgraded — for both catalog-managed models and customer-locked (pinned) models. This ticket defines the product scope, acceptance criteria, rollout policy, and required guardrails for the feature.

Why: We continue to see manual pin upgrades cause regressions in customer apps. Running lightweight, focused evals as a preflight and post-deploy step will reduce undetected regressions and enable safer rollouts. The system must be configurable (which suites to run), cost-aware (avoid runaway eval costs), and actionable (clear pass/fail gating and rollback recommendations).

Scope:
- Trigger points: catalog model version changes, customer pin upgrades, config diffs that affect runtime (quantization, tokenization, prompts-as-config).
- Eval types: smoke (latency, basic correctness), regression (production prompt set diffs), safety (toxicity/hallucination heuristics), and fidelity (embedding drift).
- Execution modes: preflight (blocking canary), post-deploy monitoring (non-blocking), and manual re-run.
- Policies: configurable thresholds per customer or global defaults; catalog upgrades will run preflight by default; customer-pinned upgrades will default to opt-in for self-serve customers and mandatory for enterprise with SLA.

Acceptance criteria:
1) When a catalog model version is promoted, the system queues a preflight eval job and reports results in the Console rollout UI with pass/fail status and metric diffs.
2) Admins can specify which eval suites run per model or per customer, with sensible defaults.
3) Failing preflight blocks canary rollout and surfaces mitigation options (rollback, reduced temperature/quant, alternate model).
4) All triggered eval runs appear in the Evals dashboard and are linked to the model-pin event, PR, and any ongoing rollout.
5) Alerts created for P0 regressions (e.g., >5% accuracy delta on critical prompt sets) notify on-call and the release owner.

Design & decisions so far:
- We will use ephemeral eval runners (separate from the hosted inference fleet) to avoid noisy neighbor effects on production. Pros: predictable cost accounting and isolation. Cons: additional infra cost; mitigated by scheduling and sample-size heuristics.
- Default eval sample sizes will be small for initial preflight (30–100 prompts) and larger for post-deploy (1k prompts over 24h) to catch regressions with statistical power.
- Catalog vs customer behavior: catalog upgrades run automatically; customer-pinned changes are opt-in except for customers on enterprise plans (decision based on security/compliance needs).
- Failure thresholds: conservative by default (e.g., >10% regression in utility metrics for non-critical suites, >3% for critical prompt sets). Thresholds are configurable per-customer and tied to the rollout policy.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) API and events: extend Catalog & Pin APIs to emit structured events (model_pin.change, model_pin.upgrade) with metadata about config diffs. (ENG- task will own schema)
2) Orchestration: build Eval Triggerer service to translate events to eval runs. Support tags to select eval suites.
3) Evals infra: add ephemeral runner provisioning and budgeting controls; integrate with existing eval harness.
4) Console: show preflight results in rollouts page, add one-click accept/rollback from results, and surface metric deltas and example prompt outputs.
5) Alerts & Integrations: wire failing preflight to PagerDuty and create GitHub PR comments linking to failed evals for model PRs.

Metrics of success:
- 50% reduction in post-upgrade regression incidents for catalog upgrades in first quarter post-launch.
- >80% coverage of catalog upgrades with preflight runs.
- <10% false-positive blocking rate (teams can override)

Dependencies: ["ENG-13550","PM-11024","ENG-2411"]

Links & artifacts:
- PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/QA/Automated+Eval+Triggers
- Design notes: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/eval-triggers/design-v1.pdf
- Eval harness repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/eval-harness (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/eval-harness/pull/3124)
- Console mockups: https://figma.com/file/abcd1234/rollout-eval-ui
- Incident reference: https://internal.redwood.ai/incidents/2025-09-upgrade-regression

Open trade-offs / questions:
- Cost vs coverage: how many sample prompts for preflight vs post-deploy (proposal: tiered sample size by customer plan).
- Opt-in behavior: whether self-serve customers should have automated preflight off by default. Initial product decision: automated for catalog; customer pinned auto-run only for enterprise.
- Runtime-sensitive config diffs: which diffs should trigger mandatory preflight (proposal: quantization level change, tokenizer changes, model architecture swap).

Next steps:
- ENG to prototype Event->Triggerer and ephemeral runner (sprint 2026-W10).
- PM to publish customer-facing docs + opt-in UI copy.
- Launch plan meeting w/ Sales & Solutions to define how we surface to enterprise customers (target: 2026-03-24).

title: Rolling capacity triage guardrails and scope reserve
preview:
Background:
Redwood's growth and increasing enterprise demand are stretching cycle capacity. Teams report mid-cycle churn from unplanned incidents, urgent customer escalations, and cross-functional rollouts which erode committed scope. We need standard, repeatable guardrails for assigning a rolling scope reserve and triaging mid-cycle work so that delivery predictability improves without blocking critical incidents.

Goal:
Define a lightweight, repeatable process and decision rubric that: 1) allocates a configurable reserve of team capacity each cycle; 2) standardizes triage steps for mid-cycle requests (feature changes, production incidents, partner needs); 3) provides explicit rules for scope cuts and redeployment of reserves during surges.

Success metrics:
- Cycle commitment hit rate (stories completed / stories committed) increases by 20% within two cycles after rollout.
- Mean unplanned work hours per engineer per cycle falls by 35%.
- Number of emergency roll-forward items requiring cross-team rework falls by 50%.

Proposed approach (high level):
1) Capacity Reserve Model: teams pick one of three reserve profiles when finalizing cycle commitments: 
   - Conservative (10% reserve): recommended for teams with high external dependencies or production-facing services.
   - Balanced (6% reserve): default for most product teams.
   - Aggressive (3% reserve): for mature, low-dependency squads aiming for high throughput.
   Reserve is expressed as story-point buffer or FTE-days depending on team preference.

2) Triage Rubric for mid-cycle work (triage owner: on-call PM + Eng lead):
   - Severity assessment: Customer-facing outage (P0), customer-impacting bug (P1), internal process improvement (P2), feature change request (P3).
   - Routing: P0 -> immediate interrupt, P1 -> evaluate for reserve consumption (if within reserve, accept; else escalate to Product Council), P2/P3 -> backlog with promised review window (5 business days).
   - Cost of fix vs business impact: use simple ROI worksheet for P1 decisions.

3) Scope-cut ladder (how to reclaim capacity when reserve is exhausted):
   - Step 1: Pause low-risk stretch goals (label: stretch).
   - Step 2: Defer listed non-blocking stories with clear fallbacks (assign owners for follow-up).
   - Step 3: If still urgent, invoke cross-team trade with counter-offers (exchange runway for prioritized work).

4) Governance and tooling:
   - Add "cycle-reserve-profile" metadata to Linear commitments for reporting.
   - Dashboards in Console showing reserve utilization and unplanned work by team.
   - Weekly mid-cycle checkpoint meeting template (15 min) for red/amber/green status per team.

Out-of-scope:
- Overhauling team-level estimation methodologies.
- Mandating a single reserve percentage for all teams.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Teams underreport unplanned work to game metrics. Mitigation: integrate automated incident logs and corroborate with on-call entries.
- Risk: Cross-team dependencies make reserve choices ineffective. Mitigation: require dependency mapping during planning; escalate to platform capacity pool if multiple teams are impacted.

Implementation plan and milestones:
- Week 0 (planning): Finalize rubric and reserve profiles, ship Linear metadata field (ENG-22111).
- Week 1 (pilot): Run with 6 volunteer teams (mix infra, product, and platform).
- Week 3 (iteration): Gather pilot feedback, refine dashboard widgets and checkpoint template.
- Week 5 (rollout): Announce org-wide, include playbook and short training for PMs and ENG leads.

Deliverables:
- Playbook doc with decision rubric and cut ladder (Confluence link attached).
- Linear templates + metadata field definition.
- Console dashboard for reserve utilization and unplanned work.
- Training slide deck and 30-minute office hours session for PMs and eng leads.

Acceptance criteria:
- The reserve metadata is available in Linear for all teams.
- Pilot teams report usage of the triage rubric in at least two mid-cycle events.
- Dashboard shows reserve utilization data and flags teams >75% reserve consumption.
- Training materials posted and at least one session delivered before rollout.

title: Release documentation orchestration and stakeholder traffic control
preview:
Objective: Produce coordinated, audience-tailored release artifacts (customer-facing changelog, admin changelog, internal one-pager, and exec summary) and orchestrate an efficient, auditable stakeholder review flow so the release communications can ship on the planned date without last-minute redactions or compliance issues.

Scope:
- Draft two customer-facing changelogs: 'Consumer' (short, benefits-focused) and 'Admin' (detailed breaking changes, migration steps, RBAC/KMS notes).
- Create an internal release brief for support, sales, and account teams including rollout schedule, anticipated tickets, and escalation contacts.
- Produce an executive summary (1-pager) for leadership and post-release metrics owners.
- Run a cross-functional signoff loop with Engineering, Design, Security, Legal, Docs, Solutions Eng, and GTM.
- Localize customer-facing changelogs for EN, FR, DE; queue to translation vendor after signoff.
- Ensure embargoed timeline and gated publish actions in the newsroom and product docs.

Key decisions so far:
- Split changelogs into Consumer + Admin to reduce noise for standard users and surface necessary operational details for platform customers.
- Use the pre-existing release template stored in Confluence and the redaction checklist (security/PII/legal) before publishing.
- Automate signoff reminders through Slack #release-signoff and the @changelog-bot; manual signoff is required from Security and Legal for any changes that touch audit/logging or encryption text.

Deliverables:
- customer-facing changelog (short) – google drive draft link in field below.
- admin changelog (detailed) – drive link below.
- internal brief (confluence) – link below.
- exec one-pager (PDF) – to be uploaded to drive after final review.

Risk / Mitigations:
- Risk: Late legal redaction requiring last-minute edits. Mitigation: have Legal triage window 48–72 hours before publish; tag the specific paragraphs that require legal review.
- Risk: Localization delays. Mitigation: freeze English final 72 hours before publish and send to vendor; use priority pass for critical languages.

Tools & links:
- Drafts: https://drive.google.com/example/release-2026-03-consumer (consumer draft), https://drive.google.com/example/release-2026-03-admin (admin draft)
- Internal brief: https://confluence.company.internal/release-playbooks/2026-03-brief
- Signoff tracking board: https://linear.app/redwood/board/release-signoffs
- GitHub PR for docs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/docs/pull/981
- Release notes template: https://confluence.company.internal/templates/release-notes-template

Owner: PM to coordinate content; Design to produce short visual changelog headers; Engineering to validate technical change descriptions and callouts; Legal & Security to approve text that references encryption, audit, or customer data handling.

Acceptance criteria: see acceptance_criteria field.

title: Scheduled tenant-scoped compliance bundles with residency-aware delivery
preview:
Problem: Enterprise customers (SOC2/ISO-regulated) need scheduled, verifiable evidence bundles (PDF/CSV) that respect tenant data residency and retention policies. Current ad-hoc exports from the console are manual, region-agnostic, and do not include signed audit manifests or automatic retention-based pruning.

Scope: Provide a first-class scheduled export feature for the Console that produces time-boxed compliance bundles per tenant. Bundles must: (1) be scoped to tenant(s) and their residency setting, (2) obey retention windows (exclude records beyond tenant retention), (3) include a signed manifest (SHA256 checksums) and optional PGP signing, (4) support PDF summary + CSV raw exports, (5) be deliverable via region-aware S3 endpoints or secure download links, and (6) surface status in a new Compliance Exports dashboard with run history, success/failure reasons, and replay.

Out of scope for MVP: data transformation pipelines beyond CSV/PDF, calendar-linked on-prem delivery (separate Private deployment work), and long-term archival lifecycle management (handled by retention/archival SLA project).

Out of scope for MVP: data transformation pipelines beyond CSV/PDF, calendar-linked on-prem delivery (separate Private deployment work), and long-term archival lifecycle management (handled by retention/archival SLA project).

title: Stability Fingerprint & Actionable Observability for Model Rollouts
preview: Build a 'Stability Fingerprint' experience in Console that surfaces per-cohort health and causality signals for canary rollouts, A/B tests, and shadow traffic. The feature bundles cohort-level dashboards, side-by-side metric diffs, trace links, per-route distributions (latency, p99, error rates), and automated recommendations for safe actions (pause, ramp, rollback). It should integrate with the rollout policy engine to enable threshold-based auto-rollback and dry-run modes, and provide explainable signal-attribution so engineers can quickly determine whether regressions are model-quality, infra, or traffic-related.

title: Cohort Storyboard + Rollback Policy Editor for rollout observability
preview:
Problem: Engineers and SREs running canary/A-B/ shadow rollouts lack a single, narrative-driven view that ties per-route metrics, request traces, and cohort diffs together with an editable auto-rollback policy. Current Console panels surface metric tables and simple rollouts, but teams spend manual effort correlating trace samples, error fingerprints, and traffic-slice differences before deciding to roll back.

Goal: Build a "Cohort Storyboard" in Console — a timeline-first, side-by-side comparison that links cohorts to sample traces, per-route token/latency/cost breakdowns, and a visual rollback policy editor (thresholds, hysteresis, and staged actions). The feature should also enable hypothesis annotations and exportable snapshots for postmortems.

Scope: MVP focuses on HTTP/text-generation routes and the most common rollout types: canary (percent-based), A/B (split), and shadow. Integrations: runtime telemetry (per-route metrics), tracing service (sample link + context), and rollout control plane for applying policy-driven auto-rollbacks or fast-fallbacks.

Out of scope for MVP: full cohort statistical testing engine, UI for multi-model cascades, and on-prem only features (will follow in Private deployment phase).
